title: Past perfect simple, past perfect continuous
description: Learn how to use the past perfect simple and past perfect continuous to describe actions and situations that happened before another point in the past.

topics.title: Past perfect simple
topics.description: Used for an action or situation that happened before another point or action in the past, and for completed actions whose result mattered at that earlier past time.

columns: I / you / he / she / it / we / they

uses.usage: An action or state that happened before a point in the past
uses.example: I'd finished my homework a few minutes before the lesson started.

uses.usage: A completed action, emphasising the result at a point in the past
uses.example: We were happy because we'd all done our homework.

notes.content: The past perfect simple is often used with the following words and phrases.
examples: By: I'd finished my homework by eight o'clock.
By the time: By the time I got to class, the lesson had started.
Before: The teacher had checked the answers before the lesson.
After: I left after I'd finished the test.
Just: Simon had just finished the test when the bell rang.
When: I left when I'd finished the test.

notes.content: Using the past simple or the past perfect simple can change the order of events in a sentence.
examples: Past simple: The lesson started when I arrived. (I arrived, then the lesson started.)
Past perfect simple: The lesson had started when I arrived. (The lesson started, then I arrived.)

notes.content: Some verbs have irregular past participle forms.

topics.title: Past perfect continuous
topics.description: Used for an action that had been continuing up to a point in the past, or that had stopped only a short time before that point.

columns: I / you / he / she / it / we / they

uses.usage: An action that had been continuing up to a point in the past
uses.example: We'd been doing grammar exercises for over an hour, so we were really bored!

uses.usage: An action that had just stopped before a point in the past
uses.example: They had a break because they'd been working so hard.

notes.content: The past perfect continuous is often used with the following words and phrases.
examples: For: Tony had been studying for hours, so he had a headache.
Since: She'd been hoping to win the competition since the summer.
Before: We'd been talking about the Internet before the lesson started.
All day/night: I'd been studying all day.

notes.content: The past perfect simple often emphasises the result or completion of an action.
examples: She'd written an article for the school newspaper.
This means that she had finished the article.

notes.content: The past perfect continuous often emphasises the process and the time spent on the action, not necessarily the result.
examples: She'd been writing an article for the school newspaper.
This means that she had started the article, but she may not have finished it yet.
